terms.definition: A structured prompt framework: Meta, Input, Output, Reasoning, Quality, Advanced.
Model settings: temperature, reasoning depth, response length, repetition control.
An AI system that observes the environment, makes decisions, and learns from outcomes; the main actor in reinforcement learning.
A system that performs tasks in steps and can use tools such as search, APIs or files. Unlike a simple chat, it can take several actions and use external sources.
Asks the AI to take action – search, use tools (Browse, Search, API), gather data; the AI acts as an agent; requires a platform with tool/search support (e.g. “find and list TOP10”).
Long-term context the AI “remembers” across conversations – your preferences, style, past decisions.
A metaphor: the world of AI as a hierarchy – from simple rules to generative AI and tools. Helps you see where sources, context, and tools “fit”; the deeper the level, the more powerful the options.
A ready-made end result – a report, Custom GPT, webpage, or other tangible output created with AI.
A language model that generates text word by word – each next word depends on the previous ones; this is how ChatGPT, Claude, and other LLMs work.
A short brief before you start: topic, audience, goal, scope, and tone – the basis for a structured prompt.
A sequence of tools or steps (e.g. Claude → ChatGPT → Gamma). A workflow chain is when several AI tools are used one after another for a single result.
A logical piece of text into which a document is split for a RAG system – helps the AI find and use only relevant information.
A prompt type that combines several methods in one prompt – role + steps + comparison + format; the most powerful but also the most complex.
A compact set of sources (summaries, facts, quotes) that fits in one prompt and gives the AI the context it needs.
When the AI “forgets” earlier information in a long conversation – attention fades, and information in the middle loses impact (Lost in the Middle).
Deliberate, structured presentation of information to the AI – what data, in what order, how much – so that the answer is accurate and useful.
The maximum amount of input and output text (in tokens) the model can “see” at once; beyond that, the oldest information is dropped.
A multi-agent role that breaks down a task, assigns specialists and merges their outputs.
A ChatGPT project with rules, instructions, and files – a personal or team assistant for a specific task.
The part of machine learning that uses multi-layer neural networks – the basis for image, language, and text AI models.
In-depth research with AI – several steps (breaking down questions, searching sources, synthesising into one answer or report).
Block that provides concrete data: numbers, dates, metrics – the facts the AI needs for the task.
Quiz or test to verify understanding before moving to practice.
Questions phrased to steer the answer in a desired direction; a form of prompt manipulation to avoid – use neutral prompts instead.
Large Language Model – a large language model; the technical basis for AI that processes text and can use tools (Tool Use) and memory (Retrieval).
The area of AI where the system learns from data without being explicitly programmed – it finds patterns and makes predictions.
A personalised prompt about you or your organisation (who you are, what you do, goals, style, audience), giving the AI context; differs from a system prompt, which defines how the AI should behave.
Block that defines who you are (role), what experience, and who the result is for (audience).
Specifies how to do the task – role, steps, format; the model produces an answer from the method without needing external search or tools (e.g. an analysis report).
A system where several roles, such as coordinator, specialist and evaluator, work together and pass clear inputs and outputs to each other.
The AI’s ability to work with several formats at once – text, images, audio, video; e.g. upload a photo and get a description.
A computing model inspired by brain neurons – the basis for deep learning and modern AI models.
Block that specifies the format and structure of the result: table, list, language, length.
When several tasks or steps are done at the same time (not one after another). The opposite of a chain – a chain is step by step.
A concrete business situation where you apply the 6-block system to create a prompt.
A repeatable sequence of steps; a clear process gives clarity and consistent results. Differs from a single chain – here it’s the overall way of working with AI.
A process-based request – asking to create or describe a process (strategy, project management, operations improvement); brings clarity for teams and decision-makers.
A personal or team store of templates – prompts organised by task and use case so you don’t have to write from scratch.
Using strategically worded prompts to influence AI answers (bias, leading questions, etc.); something to avoid – use neutral prompts.
Keeping prompts in versions (v1, v2, v3) – what worked, what didn’t, what you changed; helps improve and avoid repeating mistakes.
In the 6-block system – quality requirements: sources, citing, “say I don’t know” rule, objectivity, limits.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation – a method where the AI generates the answer using retrieved information from documents or databases, not only from its training.
A numeric signal showing whether an AI action was good or bad; the main mechanism in reinforcement learning.
An agent loop principle: understand the task, choose a tool, act, observe the result and decide whether to repeat or finish.
Reinforcement Learning – the AI learns from trial and error by receiving a numeric reward (without human scoring).
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback – reinforcement learning with human feedback; used to shape “better” answer behaviour in AI models.
A multi-agent role that classifies a request and sends it to the right specialist or workflow.
A supervised training stage – people prepare example question–answer pairs, and the model learns to respond in the desired style.
The smallest unit of text the AI processes; roughly ~4 characters (EN); determines context window size and cost.
A function the AI can call, such as search, a spreadsheet, file reading or an API. Not all platforms provide the same tools.
The AI uses external tools – search, API calls, calculations, retrieval from a database; needed for agentic prompts and RAG.
Third normal form – a database structure rule that reduces duplication and helps connect tables more clearly.
A fast temporary data layer for sessions, queues, or speed. Usually an addition to the main database, not the main storage.
Single-screen report with key metrics, charts, and trends – status at a glance. AI can help generate dashboard structure or copy.
Sequential data path: collection → preparation → exploratory analysis → models → visualization → publishing. Each step has its own AI prompts.
Data preparation: remove duplicates, fill missing values, unify formats. Required before analysis; AI can suggest cleaning steps and prompts.
Exploratory data analysis – first step before models: see what is in the data, patterns, outliers. AI can help with stats, correlations, and hypotheses.
Entity-Relationship model – a description of tables and their relationships, such as 1-N or N-N, that helps understand data structure.
A multi-agent role that checks output against criteria, rules and risks; it can return work for revision.
A connection between AI and external data or services such as APIs, databases, email or CRM. Required for agents and workflow automation.
Key performance indicator – what you measure to see whether a business process is working well.
A database approach without a strict table schema. Useful for documents, flexible structure, and fast MVPs.
A database based on tables and relationships. Useful for structured business data, queries, and reports.
Combining several sources or research outputs into one clear set of conclusions or summaries. AI helps merge different answers into one view.
Prompt section listing data or information sources (pages, reports, official data). Reduces errors and improves answer grounding.
Presenting data in charts, tables, or diagrams – to understand trends and conclusions. AI can generate visualization specs or code.
An event that starts a workflow, such as a new email, form submission or uploaded file.
Real-time data transfer between systems: an event immediately sends data to a specified address, usually through an API call.
A sequence of steps or tools with a clear output. It can connect several AI tools or business systems into one flow.
